file_name: Yasmin and Sathik - 2015 - An improved iterative segmentation algorithm using canny edge detector for skin lesion border detect.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an improved iterative segmentation algorithm with a Canny edge detector for skin lesion border detection, which is a computer vision/image processing technique used to aid in the diagnosis of melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an 'improved iterative segmentation algorithm' and explicitly states in the abstract and introduction that 'experimental results demonstrate the successful border detection' and that the performance is 'compared with the segmentation algorithm... developed by us previously'. It details a proposed methodology, steps for implementation, and validation on real skin lesion images, which characterizes it as an original experimental study rather than a review.

title: An Improved Iterative Segmentation Algorithm using Canny Edge Detector for Skin Lesion Border Detection
journal: The International Arab Journal of Information Technology
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of accurate skin lesion border detection for early melanoma diagnosis by proposing an improved iterative segmentation algorithm utilizing a Canny edge detector. The authors implemented this method in MATLAB and tested it on real clinical skin lesion images artificially corrupted with 10% salt and pepper noise to simulate realistic conditions. The proposed algorithm iteratively refines the border detection by feeding previous edge-detected images back into the Canny detector, contrasting with a previous non-iterative approach by the same authors. Experimental results demonstrated that the improved iterative method successfully segmented lesion borders even in the presence of noise, whereas the previous algorithm failed on several noisy images. The study concludes that the proposed iterative approach is more reliable and effective for border detection of noisy skin lesions compared to the previously developed segmentation algorithm.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on skin lesion border detection to aid in the early diagnosis of melanoma. The abstract and introduction explicitly state that the algorithm helps in the early detection of malignant melanoma and that accurate segmentation is critical for melanoma diagnosis. The study uses real skin lesion images, which are implied to include melanomas given the context of the disease's prevalence and the diagnostic goal, although specific counts or a multi-class breakdown are not provided.

The paper mentions that melanomas are hard to distinguish from common moles, even for experienced dermatologists. This distinction is cited as the motivation for the need for accurate border detection and computer-aided diagnosis, but the study does not appear to perform a classification task between moles and melanomas, but rather focuses on the segmentation of lesion borders generally, with melanoma as the primary clinical target.

all_task_types.details: The paper proposes an 'improved iterative segmentation algorithm using canny edge detector' specifically for 'skin lesion border detection'. The task involves delineating the contour of pigmented skin lesions by grouping pixels and detecting edges to separate the lesion from the surrounding skin. The output is a segmented border/mask, not a diagnostic class label or continuous numeric score.

datasets_used.name: Clinical skin lesion images (unspecified public/private source)
datasets_used.description: A collection of real clinical skin lesion images used to test the proposed iterative segmentation algorithm. The images were artificially corrupted with 10% salt and pepper noise to simulate realistic conditions. Specific source or public dataset name is not provided in the text, though references [9] and [3] are cited in figure captions for some original images.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the border detection performance of the proposed improved iterative segmentation algorithm and compare it with a previous algorithm [16].
use.other: Artificially corrupted with 10% salt and pepper noise for testing robustness.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated qualitatively and by comparing the 'ratio of well segmented Lesions' between the proposed method and a previous algorithm, without statistical inference.

Clinical skin lesion images (11 images,.metric: Ratio of well segmented lesions (qualitative success/failure)

Proposed improved iterative segmentation.result: Successfully detected the border of the noisy skin lesions in all 11 cases (images 1-11). The algorithm converged at varying iteration counts for each image: Image 1 (iteration 1), Image 2 (iteration 6), Image 3 (iteration 4), Image 4 (iteration 8), Image 5 (iteration 5), Image 6 (iteration 5), Image 7 (iteration 8), Image 8 (iteration 4), Image 9 (iteration 4), Image 10 (iteration 5), Image 11 (iteration 10).

Segmentation algorithm using Canny edge.result: Failed to delineate the boundary for original skin lesion images 1, 2, 4, and 11 (shown in Figure 4-a, b, d, and k). Poorly delineated boundaries for other images.

Clinical skin lesion images (11 images,.notes: The performance comparison chart (Figure 6) indicates that the proposed algorithm's performance is better than the segmentation algorithm using Canny edge detector [16]. The proposed method segments the lesion from the image even in the presence of noise for a variety of lesions and skin types, whereas the previous algorithm failed on several noisy images.

architecture_used.main_model: Improved iterative segmentation algorithm using Canny edge detector

steps: Add 10% salt and pepper noise (for testing robustness)
Apply median filter for background noise reduction
Convert to black and white image based on threshold
Apply Canny edge detector to find edges
Iteratively feed edge-detected images back into the Canny edge detector to trace the border
architecture_details.iteration_count: Variable per image (converged between iteration 1 and 10)

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Segmented border/mask of the skin lesion

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Improved iterative segmentation algorithm using Canny edge detector
Segmentation algorithm using Canny edge detector [16] (Yasmin et al. previous work)
preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RGB images to grayscale
Addition of 10% salt and pepper noise (artificial corruption for testing robustness)
Application of median filter for background noise reduction
Conversion to binary (black and white) image based on threshold

data_augmentation_techniques.details: 10% salt and pepper noise was added to the grayscale images to simulate realistic conditions and test the robustness of the segmentation algorithm. This was applied to the test set of 11 clinical skin lesion images.

conclusions: The proposed improved iterative segmentation algorithm using a Canny edge detector successfully detects the borders of noisy real skin lesions across various lesion types and skin types.

The proposed iterative method is more reliable and effective for border detection of noisy skin lesions compared to the previously developed non-iterative segmentation algorithm using a Canny detector, which failed to delineate boundaries for several noisy images.
The iterative approach effectively segments lesions from the background even in the presence of 10% salt and pepper noise, demonstrating robustness against image corruption.

Accurate border detection is critical for the early diagnosis of malignant melanoma, as it enables the quantitative evaluation of features such as diameter, asymmetry, and irregularity, which are difficult to assess subjectively.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small, unspecified dataset of only 11 clinical skin lesion images, limiting statistical power and generalizability.
The dataset was artificially corrupted with 10% salt and pepper noise to test robustness, which may not fully represent the complexity of real-world clinical image artifacts.
The proposed algorithm was compared only against a previous non-iterative method by the same authors [16], lacking comparison with state-of-the-art or other contemporary segmentation algorithms.
No external validation was performed; the algorithm was tested solely on the internal set of 11 images.

The paper does not report standard quantitative segmentation metrics (e.g., Dice coefficient, Jaccard index, Hausdorff distance), relying instead on a qualitative 'ratio of well segmented lesions' and visual inspection.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing or significance levels were reported to support the claim of superior performance.
The source of the clinical images is not explicitly defined as a public benchmark dataset, raising concerns about reproducibility and selection bias.
The algorithm's convergence time varies significantly per image (1 to 10 iterations), which may impact computational efficiency in clinical settings.
